paper_title: ShotcreteDepth: A Bi-modal Dataset for Robust Robotic Depth Perception in Shotcrete Construction Environments

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: We introduce ShotcreteDepth, a bi-modal dataset from the construction domain that captures both an active shotcreting process and general construction environments. The dataset comprises stereo RGB imagery and LiDAR point clouds acquired under harsh real-world conditions, including high turbidity and poor illumination. Such conditions adversely affect sensor measurements, leading to incomplete and noisy observations that pose significant challenges for perception systems in autonomous applications. Alongside the dataset, we release a lightweight annotation tool designed for time-efficient labeling of LiDAR point clouds. ShotcreteDepth consists of 11,252 temporally synchronized data samples, of which 220 are annotated for evaluation purposes. The dataset supports research in stereo matching, depth completion, and depth estimation under conditions that closely reflect the operational complexities found in industrial settings. Project repository: https://github.com/dtu-pas/shotcrete-depth

sections.title: Start
sections.content: ShotcreteDepth: Bi-modal Dataset for Robust Robotic Depth Perception in Shotcrete Construction Environments Jakub Gregorek1,2*, Lars Arnold Dethlefsen1,2, Patrick Schmidt1,2, Mads Essenbæk3, Jonas Flink Bentzen3 and Lazaros Nalpantidis1,2 6 2 0 2 2 2 ] . [ 1 2 5 1 3 2 . 6 0 6 2 : r Fig. 1: Data samples from our ShotcreteDepth dataset. RGB images originate from Roboception rc visard 160c camera, the disparity and confidence is computed internally by the camera, and point cloud from the Velodyne LiDAR projected to the image plane. Red color denotes the smallest and blue the largest values. Abstract We introduce ShotcreteDepth, bi-modal dataset from the construction domain that captures both an active shotcreting process and general construction environments. The dataset comprises stereo RGB imagery and LiDAR point clouds acquired under harsh real-world conditions, including high turbidity and poor illumination. Such conditions adversely affect sensor measurements, leading to incomplete and noisy observations that pose significant challenges for perception systems in autonomous applications. Alongside the dataset, we release lightweight annotation tool designed for time-efficient labeling of LiDAR point clouds. ShotcreteDepth consists of 11,252 temporally synchronized data samples, of which 220 are annotated for evaluation purposes. The dataset supports research in stereo matching, depth completion, and depth estimation under conditions that closely reflect the operational complexities found in industrial settings. Project repository: https://github.com/dtu-pas/shotcrete-depth I. INTRODUCTION We are publishing bi-modal datasetShotcreteDepth capturing shotcreting environment, shown in Fig. 1, aimed at development and evaluation of stereo matching, depth completion and depth estimation methods. Furthermore, we 1Technical University of Denmark, Department of Electrical and Pho2Pioneer Centre for AI, Copenhagen, Denmark. tonics Engineering. 3Christiansen & Essenbæk A/S. *Corresponding author jagre@dtu.dk. This work has been funded and supported by the EU Horizon Europe project RoBetArme under the Grant Agreement 101058731. We express our gratitude to Søren Beyer Nielsen for designing and 3D printing the dustproof sensor housing. are releasing light-weight annotation tool for LiDAR point clouds. The application of sprayed concrete, shotcrete, is construction method which can be used for reinforcing unstable structures in underground mining or tunnel constructions, building complex geometries or repairing damaged structures. The technology is mechanized to the large extend, yet the shotcrete application is still laborious work performed manually. The motivation to automate this process is rooted in achieving more consistent quality, ensuring compliance with the design requirements, reducing material and water waste by eliminating over-spraying, and limiting exposure of human workers to harmful quick-setting agents [33]. The full automation of the shotcreting process has many aspects [2], [12], [19], [27], [48], [49], [67], [68], one of which is advanced perception for autonomous systems, which are to perform the application of shotcrete. This work is aimed at the depth perception, which is relevant for navigation, mapping, obstacle avoidance, safety, measuring material deposition, compliance and process monitoring. The shotcreting environment, characterized by turbidity, poses challenge for robotic perception systems. The rebound phenomenon produces significant amount of airborne shotcrete dust concentrating in the enclosed environments. The dust limits the visibility, negatively impacting camera based depth sensing, and causes laser scattering, challenging active depth sensors like LiDARs. Development of reliable perception systems for the construction site domain, even more so for niche applications like shotcrete, suffer from lack of data. The contributions of this paper are twofold and aiming to narrow this gap: We are releasing shotcreting dataset capturing niche construction environment with the emphasis on depth perception. We are using this dataset to test various stereo, depth completion, and depth estimation baselines to elucidate its characteristics. We are providing light-weight annotation tool for 3D point clouds allowing users to label unreliable depth measurements. II. RELATED WORK A. Relevant Datasets Only few construction-related datasets contain any form of depth data, but they target variety of tasks: SLAM [55], segmentation [11], [22], [24], [30], place recognition [34], pose estimation [61], 3D reconstruction [22], object detection [11], depth estimation [11]. When it comes to shotcreting or tasks related to the shotcreting process, existing datasets aim at segmentation [50], structural performance [54] and deformations [10]. Similar harsh environmental conditions to certain extent, including turbidity, haziness, excessive or insufficient illumination can be observed in [1], [6], [13]. To our knowledge, there is no existing dataset capturing the niche shotcreting domain allowing development and evaluation of depth perception methods such as depth estimation, completion and stereo matching. B. Depth Estimation Depth estimation is the task of estimating depth based on provided monocular image. Considering the data scarcity in this niche field, our interest is mainly on the methods that, have been evaluated in the zero-shot context. Ranftl et al. [47] pioneered the zero-shot monocular depth estimation. Many of the recent depth estimation models are based on Vision Transformer (ViT) backbones [7], [37], [45], [46], [57], [58], [65], [66] initialized from DINOv2 [43], and pretrained ConvNext and ResNet backbones [47], [69]. Others methods take the generative approach [14], [18], [20], [28], [62], [70], [70]. The depth estimation models may predict disparity [20], [47], [65], [66], relative depth estimates [18], [28], [37], [57], [62], [70], metric depth [5], [7], [45], [46], [58], [69], or can be fine-tuned for metric depth [37], [65], [66]. Whereas real-world labeled datasets are usually required to train the depth estimation models [45][47], [69], some methods can take advantage of unlabeled data [37], [65], [66] and recently synthetic datasets have also started playing an important role [7], [18], [20], [28], [45], [57], [58], [62], [70]. TABLE I: ShotcreteDepth dataset parameters. Parameter Description/Value Stereo Camera Field of View Image Resolution Disparity Resolution Roboception rc visard 160 color Horizontal: 61, Vertical: 48 1280 960 640 480 LiDAR Field of View Depth Resolution Total Dataset Size Annotated Dataset Size Velodyne PUCK Vertical: 30 1280 11252 220 matching architectures [3]. Some of the recent state-of-theart methods formulate the problem as test-time optimization. Ke et al. [29] applies low-rank adaptation and visual prompt tuning. Prompt tuning was also explored by Jeong et al. [25] and low-rank adaptation by Seo et al. [51]. Diffusion-based methods [16], [23], [56] use the sparse depth to iteratively guide the diffusion process. Faster inference compared to the test-time optimization can be achieved by fine-tuning the diffusion model [17]. In contrast, Bartolomei et al. [3] utilizes virtual pattern projector and re-trains stereo matching network [40] achieving strong zero-shot generalization. Liang et al. [35] proposes distillation framework leveraging supervision of the strong depth estimators, whereas Lin et al. [39] performs super-resolution targeting noisy lowresolution sensors. Finally, Zuo and Deng [71] proposes an approach based on gated recurrent units iteratively refining depth gradients, depth integration and SPN enhancement. That work was further extended [72] introducing multiresolution depth integrator and Laplacian loss. D. Stereo Matching Stereo matching is the task of finding correspondences between images captured by stereo camera allowing to estimate depth of the observed scene. While Semi-Global Matching by Hirschmuller [21] became an industrial standard due to its favorable accuracy-efficiency trade-off [42], the deep learning methods have dominated the task. Some of the methods aggregate cost volume [31], [52], [53], [64], which is often memory expensive. Other methods perform recurrent disparity refinement [8], [36], [40], [63], which is less timeefficient. An alternative approach avoiding the construction of cost-volume is utilizing attention mechanisms establishing correspondences between stereo images [59]. The most recent methods combine the mentioned approaches [4], [26], [60] and simultaneously take advantage of the monocular foundational depth models [43], [66]. III. DATASET C. Depth Completion A. Overview In the context of this paper we define depth completion to be the task of densifying sparse depth, guided by monocular image. Zero-shot capable depth completion methods may benefit from the pre-trained priors of the depth estimators [16], [17], [23], [25], [29], [39], [51], [56] or stereo Our dataset captures the environment where the shotcreting is performed, including scenes before, during and after shotcreting, as well as the general construction environment. The dataset is bi-modal, aiming to provide data for development and evaluation of depth perception systems in conditions with challenging environmental conditions, including high turbidity and far-from-optimal illumination. It contains 11,252 temporally synchronized data samples, of which 220 are annotated for evaluation purposes. Selection of the evaluation set maximizes diversity, skipping similar consecutive frames. An overview of the datasets parameters are presented in Tab. I. B. Environment The construction site at which the dataset was collected exemplifies the type of environment autonomous systems will need to operate in, should this demanding task be automated. The workspace was sealed off from the outside, so illumination was provided exclusively by artificial lighting, and shotcrete particles accumulated rapidly in the air. The construction environments are often confined, as shotcreting is commonly done in tunnels or mines. These factors together create particularly harsh conditions for both human workers and equipment. To protect the sensors from shotcreting particles, they were mounted inside custom 3D-printed dust-proof sensor housing, shown in Fig. 3. C. Sensors & Modalities Our bi-modal dataset was captured using two sensors: Roboception rc visard 160c stereo camera with 4mm lens and Velodyne PUCK LiDAR. The stereo camera provides RGB images of resolution 1280 960 pixels, disparity and confidence maps of resolution 640480, which are computed by proprietary implementation of Semi-Global Matching [21] running directly on the Nvidia Tegra K1 embedded in the camera. The Velodyne PUCK LiDAR measures depth up to 100m with typical accuracy up to 3cm. The LiDAR has 16 scan lines, vertical field of view of 30, and operates on 903nm wavelength. The LiDAR was mounted approximately centered above the stereo camera in our dust resistant housing. We have also performed tests with solid-state LiDAR Neuvition Titan M1 (visible at the bottom of Fig. 3, which did not seem to be well suited for this task. It struggled in the turbid environment to the extent of being unable to return any depth whatsoever. It could be explained by the longer wave-length (1550nm) it operates with or internal processing not being tuned for these conditions. D. Calibration & Synchronization The intrinsic camera parameters were estimated using in the the Roboceptions calibration tool which is built camera. The extrinsic parameters of the camera and LiDAR were obtained using Matlabs Camera LiDAR and Camera Calibration. The accurate LiDAR time-stamping was ensured by the PPS signal and NMEA messages received from GPS module. The cameras clock was synchronized using PTP with system clock of our acquisition computer (also synchronized with GPS time). Our sensor setup acquires the modalities at different frequencies: RGB images at 25Hz, stereo disparity at 3Hz and LiDAR point clouds at 10 Hz. The RGB images and disparity from the stereo camera were matched with the temporally closest point cloud producing only complete sets containing all modalities (cf. Fig. 1). Fig. 3: Our 3D printed dust-proof sensor housing containing the Roboception rc visard 160 stereo camera and the Velodyne PUCK LiDAR. E. Influence of Turbidity Visibility worsens in turbid environments, which typically makes stereo matching more difficult. Nevertheless, LiDAR seems to be affected more severely and the shotcrete particles dispersed in the air seem to degrade the quality of depth measurements in situations with reasonable visibility. The impact on LiDAR is three-fold: 1) Missing data. The large amount of shotcrete particles in the air may blind the LiDAR either partially or even entirely. 2) Noisy measurements. Point clouds become visually more noisy during shotcreting. 3) Dust clouds. Shotcrete particles become visible in the LiDAR point clouds, while being mostly translucent for the camera and human eyes. F. Point Cloud Annotation Tool We developed an annotation tool, allowing us to manually annotate the dust clouds of shotcrete observed by the LiDAR. The tool allows the user to assign each point in the point cloud kept by user or removed by user label. Additionally we define the labels kept by algorithm and removed by algorithm, which are to be used for annotations performed programmatically. The user interface consists of two views (depicted in Fig. 4): the first view displays the LiDAR point cloud in 3D, whereas the second view displays the projection of the point cloud on top of the left RGB picture. The user can switch modes using shortcuts to rotate or annotate the displayed point cloud following the selected operation, keeping or removing the points. Furthermore, the tool allows the user to flag the image for evaluation. We annotated only the point clouds of the dataset samples that are to be used for evaluation. The remaining point clouds are provided without annotations. G. Removing Occlusions Utilizing multiple sensors to capture the same scene inevitably leads to occlusions [9]. To filter out occluded LiDAR points we follow [3]. That method uses sliding window, that moves on top of the depth points projected to the image plane. Points at larger distance than the nearest affine-invariant predictions of the depth estimation models. The alignment was performed by minimizing the least square error: (a, b) = arg min a,b (cid:88) iΩ (a Di + Ci)2 (1) The evaluation of stereo matching models requires groundtruth depth to be converted to disparity. Furthermore, disparity originating from the stereo matching needs to be converted to depth for sampling the sparse depth maps for the depth completion methods. This can be achieved by the formula: depth = baseline focal length disparity (2) where the baseline is given by the stereo setup and focal length is determined in the camera calibration process. B. Metrics (cid:80) (cid:80) (cid:17) digi gi (cid:16) di , gi di gi Following the standard practices, for depth estimation models we report absolute relative error REL = 1 and δ1 percentage of points where max < 1.25. denotes count of pixels in an image, di are prediction pixels and gi are ground-truth pixels. For depth completion models we report root mean (cid:113) 1 idi gi2 and mean absquared error RMSE = (cid:80) solute error MAE = 1 idi gi. The values of both, RMSE and MAE, are always provided in meters. RMSE and MAE metrics in do not fully capture the models ability to produce good quality estimates, especially when the groundtruth is sparse. Thus, we also assess boundary accuracy following [32], [44] and report Pseudo Depth Boundary Error (PDBE) accuracy acc PDBE. We extract the ground-truth edges for PDBE metrics from stereo matching. For stereo matching methods we report endpoint error EPE = 1 idi gi expressed in pixels. The formulaic definition of EPE matches MAE with the exception that the prediction and ground-truth are in disparity space. Additionally, D1 is percentage of elements with error greater than 3 pixels and greater than 5% of the ground-truth disparity. For all evaluated methods we provide runtime in seconds. Timing was performed on Nvidia GeForce 4090. Model sizes are in millions of parameters. PDBE and completeness comp (cid:80) C. Models For FoundationStereo [60] we evaluated the ViT-Large checkpoint 23-51-11. For StereoAnywhere [4] we utilized the checkpoint pretrained on SceneFlow [41]. For RAFTStereo [40] we downloaded the Middlebury checkpoint recommended for in-the-wild images. For Marigold-SSD [17] we use the model trained on density range [0.16%, 5%]. Marigold-DC [56] utilizes the Marigold [28] v1.0 checkpoint. For VPP4DC [3] we used the model trained from scratch on SceneFlow [41]. For Depth Anything v3 [37], we evaluated GIANT-1.1 checkpoint. Marigold-E2E [14] weights were downloaded from HuggingFace. For MoGe2 [58] we used ViT-Large checkpoint. The depth image Fig. 4: The Annotation Tool we are releasing with the dataset. Upper image: 3D view of the LiDAR point cloud. Lower image: point cloud overlaid on top of the left camera image. The green points are kept while the purple color denotes the dust cloud which is to be excluded from evaluation data. point contained in the sliding window by given threshold are removed. Considering the lower amount of LiDAR scan lines compared to [15], we enlarge the sliding window to 10 50 pixels and set the distance threshold to 0.5m. The points marked for removal by the user are ignored in the process because removal of valid points projected in the proximity of the dust clouds is not desired. The points to be removed by the filtering method are marked as removed by algorithm in the provided annotations. IV. EXPERIMENTS We used the ShotcreteDepth dataset ourselves to test various stereo, depth completion, and depth estimation baselines. The goal of these experiments was to elucidate the characteristics of the dataset. A. Experimental Setup For all evaluated methods, inference was performed at resolution of 640 480 pixels. The predictions were then upscaled and metrics computed at the original resolution of 1280 960. The annotated and filtered LiDAR point clouds serve as the ground-truth for model evaluation. The depth completion models were provided 500 uniformly sampled depth points originating from stereo matching. The same depth points were also used to align scale and shift of the settings of the rc visard camera were set to defaults, with the exception of Quality being set to Full and enabled Double-Shot and Static. D. Stereo Matching The quantitative results evaluating the selected stereo matching methods are presented in Tab. II. In Fig. 5 we compare the methods qualitatively. While stereo matching based on Semi-global Matching [21] is optimized enough to run on embedded hardware, it often produces incomplete depth maps. The more computationally heavy-weight neural networks are more robust in the challenging environmental conditions captured in our dataset. We can observe, that the methods based on neural networks can deal with occlusions and provide more visually distinct edges on the object boundaries, even in extremely dark (cf. Fig. 5-left) or overexposed (cf. Fig. 5-middle) parts of images. E. Depth Completion The results for depth completion are presented in Tab. III. The depth methods were used to complete depth maps sampled from stereo matching. The Stereo Anywhere method was selected as the source of depth, because it achieves the lowest EPE and D1, which are the common metrics for evaluation of the matching algorithms. 500 depth points were sampled uniformly. Stereo Anywhere was also used as the source of ground-truth edges for PDBE metrics, because it produces visually cleanest output (see Fig. 5). The qualitative results including visualization of the sampled sparse depth are presented in Fig. 6. F. Depth Estimation We evaluated three depth estimation models; the results are summarized in Tab. IV. The sparse depth maps sampled for the depth completion methods in the previous subsection were used to align shift and scale of the affine invariant estimates of Depth Anything v3 [38] and Marigold-E2E [14]. Since MoGe-2 [58] predicts metric depth we evaluated it with and without shift and scale adjustment. The qualitative results are presented in Fig. 7. TABLE II: Evaluation of stereo matching methods: three neural-network based approaches (RAFT - RAFTStereo [40], FS - FoundationStereo [60], SA - Stereo Anywhere [4]) and the proprietary SGM [21] implementation running on the Roboception rc visard 160 camera. Timing was performed on Nvidia GeForce 4090, while runtime of the cameras stereo matching given by the FPS of the camera. Metrics EPE D1 MAE RMSE RAFT 2.449 0.130 0.337 0. Methods SA FS 2.439 0.129 0.327 0.741 2.328 0.103 0.311 0.795 Coverage 100% 100% 100% Parameters Runtime 11 375 347 0.307 0.156 0.141 rc visard 2.276 0.106 1.467 2. 61% - 0.333 Fig. 5: Comparing stereo matching methods. From top to bottom: left RGB image, disparity maps computed by rc visard stereo matching, RAFT-Stereo [40], FoundationStereo [60] and Stereo Anywhere [4]. TABLE III: Evaluation of three depth completion methods: Marigold-DC [56] for single run (1st value) and ensemble of 10 runs (2nd value), Marigold-SSD [17] and VPP4DC [3]. Metrics MAE RMSE acc PDBE comp PDBE Marigold-DC 0.364 / 0.325 0.893 / 0.793 1.636 / 1.189 4.687 / 5.780 Parameters Runtime 950 24.479 / 254.284 Methods Marigold-SSD VPP4DC 0.422 0.868 1.576 21.107 989 0.382 0.360 0.790 2.076 10.783 11 0.116 TABLE IV: Evaluation of depth estimation methods: DepthAnything v3 [37], Marigold-E2E [14], MoGe-2 [58] with scale and shift alignment (1st value) and without (2nd value). Metrics REL δ1 MAE RMSE Parameters Runtime DepthAnything v3 Marigold-E2E MoGe-2 Methods 0.133 0.834 0.554 0. 1356 0.095 0.174 0.727 0.715 1.016 950 0.175 0.142 / 0.210 0.816 / 0.594 0.576 / 0.832 0.879 / 1.134 326 0.057 (a) RGB (b) Sparse Depth (c) Marigold-SSD (d) Marigold-DC (e) VPP4DC Fig. 6: Qualitative results for depth completion methods. (a) RGB (b) Marigold-E2E (c) Depth Anything v3 (d) MoGe-2 Fig. 7: Qualitative results for depth estimation methods. V. DISCUSSION AND CONCLUSION Our experiments in Sec. IV, have shown that depth perception is indeed possible in the challenging conditions of shotcreting environments. Each one of the tested approaches3 stereo matching approaches, 3 depth completion methods and 3 depth estimation methodshas exhibited merits in the corresponding task. While computational efficiency and near-real-time operation is feasible, this comes at the expense of output accuracy. Contrary, large deep learning-based models are characterized by inferior runtimes (even when executed on dedicated GPU-enabled workstations), but their accuracy is typically growing with their size for all 3 considered tasks. In this work, we have introduced ShotcreteDepth, bimodal dataset for evaluation and development of depth perception methods and used it to test 9 state-of-the-art deep learning methods. Our dataset captures the niche shotcreting environment; an environment characterized by high turbidity. Additionally, we developed lightweight annotation tool for 3D point clouds, which used to remove the dust clouds observed by LiDAR from the evaluation. Stereo cameras and LiDARs are fundamentally distinct and better suited for different scenarios. We see potential to achieve the best depth perception by their fusion, which would require an additional source of depth for evaluation.
